meta.title: Wat Chiang Man · Chiang Mai’s Oldest Temple
meta.description: Wat Chiang Man (วัดเชียงมั่น), Chiang Mai’s oldest temple, founded by King Mangrai in 1296. Home of the crystal Buddha Phra Sae Tang Khamani and the elephant-backed chedis.

hero.subtitle: The oldest temple in Chiang Mai, where King Mangrai first settled when he founded the city — a serene Lanna sanctuary of whitewashed walls, teak pillars and elephant-backed stupas.
hero.boardEn: วัดเชียงมั่น · Chiang Mai, Thailand
hero.plannerNote: Golden-hour times shift with the seasons — a gentle guide, not a forecast.
hero.defaultTip: Quietest before 08:00 and after 17:00; mornings have the best light for photography.
hero.lightCaption: Light forecast uses local sun times (Asia/Bangkok).

quickFacts.mustSeeVal: Elephant chedis · Crystal Buddha · Stone Buddha

about.label: About
about.heading: Chiang Mai’s first temple
about.lead: Tucked into the quiet north of the old walled city, Wat Chiang Man is where Chiang Mai began — both as a sacred site and as a royal residence.
about.paragraph1: In 1296 King Mangrai (Mengrai) chose this spot as his base while laying out the new Lanna capital. He built a modest pavilion here and lived in it during the city’s founding — making Wat Chiang Man the oldest temple within Chiang Mai’s moat.
about.paragraph2: Centuries later the temple remains a living place of worship. Its whitewashed walls, weathered teak and small scale give it an intimacy the larger city temples lack, even as it guards two of northern Thailand’s most revered Buddha images.
about.quote: “To stand in Wat Chiang Man is to stand where Chiang Mai itself was born.”

etymology.title: What “Chiang Man” means
etymology.text: In Lanna / Thai, “Chiang” (เชียง) means “city”, and “Man” (มั่น) means “firm, enduring”. So “Chiang Man” reads as “the stable city” — King Mangrai’s wish that his new capital would endure.

highlights.title: Phra Sae Tang Khamani
highlights.desc: An 11th-century crystal Buddha believed to protect the city and bring rain.

highlights.title: Phra Sila
highlights.desc: A revered stone image linked to Sri Lanka and the rainy season.

highlights.title: Chiang Man stupas
highlights.desc: Two square stupas resting on the backs of ornate elephants.

editorTip.text: Dress modestly, remove your shoes in the halls, and aim for an early morning visit to enjoy the temple in peace.

arch.label: Architecture & Treasures
arch.heading: Sacred art of the Lanna court
arch.intro: Wat Chiang Man is a compact museum of Lanna craft — from its square elephant-backed stupas to the two small Buddhas that have drawn pilgrims for nearly a thousand years.

treasures.title: The crystal Buddha
treasures.text: Carved from a single crystal and dated to the 11th century, this small standing Buddha is also called Phra Kaew Khao ('the white jewel'). It is paraded through the old city during Songkran to invite rain and fortune.

treasures.title: The stone Buddha
treasures.text: A sandstone relief image associated with Sri Lanka, long credited with rain-making powers. Devotees still venerate it for protection and a good harvest.

architecture.title: Elephant-backed chedis
architecture.desc: Two cubical stupas (13th c.) sit on tiers of stucco elephants — the iconic 'Chiang Man' form unique to this temple, formally named Chedi Chang Lom (เจดีย์ช้างล้อม, 'stupa surrounded by elephants').

architecture.title: Viharn Luang
architecture.desc: The main hall, rebuilt in the 1920s on the original foundations; its old teak pillars are preserved inside.

architecture.title: Ubosot (ordination hall)
architecture.desc: A smaller, quieter hall used for monastic rites, with fine Lanna woodwork.

arch.noteText: The two treasures are kept in the small library-style structure (ho trai) on the right; ask a monk if you’d like a closer look.

history.label: History
history.heading: From royal camp to city shrine
history.intro: Wat Chiang Man’s story is the story of Chiang Mai’s founding — a temple that began as a king’s temporary dwelling and grew into the city’s spiritual heart.

blocks.title: Mangrai’s encampment
blocks.text: King Mangrai of the Lanna kingdom chose the site as his headquarters while founding Chiang Mai, living in a pavilion that became the temple’s nucleus.

blocks.title: The elephant chedis
blocks.text: The two square stupas on elephant backs were raised, establishing the distinctive Chiang Man architectural style.

blocks.title: Rebuilding the viharn
blocks.text: The main assembly hall was reconstructed on its original footprint, preserving the ancient teak columns within.

blocks.title: A living temple
blocks.text: Wat Chiang Man remains an active monastery and the most visited of Chiang Mai’s historic temples.

legend.title: The rain-making Buddhas
legend.text: Both Phra Sae Tang Khamani and Phra Sila are believed to control the rains. In times of drought, the crystal Buddha was once carried in procession to summon the monsoon.

didYouKnow.title: Did you know?
didYouKnow.text: Wat Chiang Man sits inside the square moat of the old city’s northwest quarter — the exact area where Mangrai first laid out his capital.

folklore.title: The crystal that calls the rain
folklore.tale: Local lore says the white crystal Buddha 'speaks' through the weather: when it is taken out for Songkran, the skies open and the rice fields drink. Farmers still watch its procession as a blessing for the year ahead.
folklore.shrine: The crystal Buddha rests in the ho trai (scripture library) on the temple’s right side, brought out for public viewing during Songkran each April.

guide.label: Visitor Guide
guide.heading: Plan a calm, respectful visit
guide.lead: Wat Chiang Man is free to enter and easy to reach on foot from anywhere in the old city. A little planning makes the experience far richer.

items.title: Temple entry
items.desc: Free. A small donation at the booth supports upkeep and the monks.

items.title: Dress code
items.desc: Shoulders and knees covered; remove shoes in all halls.

items.title: Photography
items.desc: Allowed in the grounds; avoid flash and never photograph monks without asking.

items.title: From the airport
items.desc: Chiang Mai International Airport is ~5 km from the Old City. A Grab or taxi takes 15–20 min (≈150–200 THB) straight to the Old City, then walk or hop a red songthaew to the temple.

items.title: On foot
items.desc: 10–15 min walk from Tha Phae Gate or most old-city guesthouses.

items.title: Songthaew / red truck
items.desc: Hop a red songthaew and ask for 'Wat Chiang Man' near Ratchapakhinai Rd.

items.title: Tuk-tuk / Grab
items.desc: A short, inexpensive ride from anywhere in the city centre.

photography.title: Photography guide
photography.intro: The temple’s pale walls and warm teak glow at the edges of the day.

times.desc: Soft, even light and empty courtyards — ideal for the elephant chedis.
Low sun rakes the stucco elephants and gilded halls.

spots.name: Elephant chedis
spots.view: From the courtyard, looking up at the tiers of elephants.
spots.tip: Get low for a dramatic angle on the stupa base.

spots.name: Viharn doorway
spots.tip: Wait for a monk or local to pass for scale.

settings.phone: 0.5× / 1×, tap to expose for the walls

tips: Never stand on a chedi or climb the stupas.
Step around, not over, prayer mats and offerings.
Ask before photographing people at worship.

bestTime.title: Best time to visit
bestTime.intro: Chiang Mai is warm year-round; the temple is most rewarding in the cool and dry season.

seasons.name: Best overall
seasons.desc: Pleasant mornings, clear light, and the lead-up to Songkran.

seasons.name: Quiet & bright
seasons.desc: Very hot midday — come at dawn; Songkran (Apr) brings the crystal Buddha procession.

seasons.name: Green & calm
seasons.desc: Afternoon showers, fewer crowds, lush temple gardens.

items.title: Dress modestly
items.desc: Cover shoulders and knees; a scarf or sarong helps.

items.title: Mind your belongings
items.desc: Old-city temples are safe, but keep valuables close in busy periods.

items.title: Watch the sun
items.desc: Carry water — midday heat can be intense from March to May.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: The temple grounds are flat and mostly step-free; the halls have a few thresholds.

items.title: Paths
items.desc: Paved, level walkways connect the gates, chedis and halls.

items.title: Wheelchairs
items.desc: The courtyard is navigable; hall interiors are tight — ask a monk for help if needed.

items.title: Watch on-site signs
items.desc: Core areas such as the Ubosot (ordination hall) may restrict entry or require shoe removal — follow posted signs and monk guidance.

faqs.q: How old is Wat Chiang Man?
faqs.a: It was founded in 1296 by King Mangrai, making it the oldest temple in Chiang Mai.
Yes. Donations are welcome but not required.

faqs.q: Can I see the crystal Buddha?
faqs.a: It is kept in the ho trai and shown publicly during Songkran (April); at other times, ask a monk.
30–60 minutes is enough to see the highlights at a relaxed pace.

items.name: Three Kings Monument
items.desc: The city’s symbolic founding square, 10 min walk south.

items.name: Wat Phra Singh
items.desc: A grand Lanna temple 15 min west.

items.name: Sunday Walking Street
items.desc: Chiang Mai’s famous night market, just south on Ratchadamnoen Rd.

food.label: Nearby Eats
food.heading: Eat like a local nearby
food.lead: The lanes around Wat Chiang Man are packed with old-city cafés, noodle stalls and northern-Thai kitchens.

dishes.name: Khao Soi
dishes.desc: Chiang Mai’s famous curried noodle soup — try it at a shop on Ratchaphakhinai.

dishes.name: Sai Ua
dishes.desc: Herbed northern-Thai pork sausage, grilled and served with chili.

dishes.name: Kanom Krok
dishes.desc: Coconut-rice pancakes cooked on a hot griddle, crispy outside, soft within.

dishes.name: Old-city café
dishes.desc: Quiet courtyard cafés for a slow breakfast before the temple.

quote.text: “The best khao soi in the old city is a five-minute walk from the temple gate.”
quote.author: — A Chiang Mai regular

gallery.label: Gallery
gallery.heading: Inside Wat Chiang Man
gallery.intro: A visual walk through the temple’s halls, chedis and quiet corners.
gallery.credits: Photos are illustrative of Wat Chiang Man’s architecture and setting.
alts: Teak pillars of the main hall

reviews.label: Reviews
reviews.heading: What visitors say
reviews.intro: Wat Chiang Man earns consistently high marks for its calm atmosphere and historic beauty.
reviews.disclaimer: Ratings summarise public visitor feedback and are provided for guidance only.
reviews.declaration: This site is an independent visitor guide and is not affiliated with the temple or Google.

featured.text: “The oldest and one of the most peaceful temples in the old city — the elephant chedis are unforgettable.”
“Come early. We had the courtyard to ourselves and the light on the white walls was perfect.”

items.text: Small but full of history. The crystal Buddha is mesmerising.
items.name: Anya R.

items.text: Loved the elephant stupas — unlike anything else in Chiang Mai.
items.name: Tom B.

items.text: Quiet and well kept. A lovely start to the morning.
items.name: Lin W.

sources.factText: Founding date, the crystal and stone Buddhas, and the elephant chedis follow widely published accounts of Wat Chiang Man.
sources.correctionText: If any detail looks wrong, we’d love to fix it.

mapSection.label: Map & Location
mapSection.heading: Find Wat Chiang Man
mapSection.lead: In the quiet north of the old city, on Ratchapakhinai Road, a short walk from the moat.
mapSection.address: 171 Ratchapakhinai Rd, Old City, Si Phum, Mueang Chiang Mai District, Chiang Mai 50200, Thailand
